reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: No mandatory style violations: no YAML Stacktape config, no StacktapeConfig type imports, no fenced TypeScript defineConfig blocks, no multi-command bash blocks, no comments inside shell blocks.
Opening paragraph is tight and self-contained — AI engine can quote it verbatim.
The FAQ is genuinely useful and covers the right questions: stage/region explanation for a read-only command, the S3-artifact caveat about standalone CloudFormation use, and CI usage guidance.
Examples section covers real-world patterns (cross-stage diff, cfn-lint gate) that a first-time user would actually attempt.
The `--preserveTempFiles` flag is explained with concrete context (.stacktape/[invocation-id]), grounded in the source.
Correct use of <CliCommandsApiReference> — no hand-authored flags table.
Related commands section links logically to deploy, preview-changes, and package-workloads, which are the natural next steps.
The 'Can I use the compiled template with AWS CLI?' caveat is important and well-placed in the FAQ.
problems: Structural deviation from the section instructions: the prescribed CLI page order is intro → usage → auto-generated reference → examples → related commands. The draft inserts 'When to use' and 'Important flags' sections between the usage example and the flags reference, pushing the <CliCommandsApiReference> table far down the page. A developer scanning for a specific flag must scroll through two sections and multiple code blocks before reaching it.

The 'Important flags' section partially duplicates information that already appears in the <CliCommandsApiReference> table below it. The three flags explained (outFile, preserveTempFiles, configPath) each appear again in the table with full descriptions. This creates redundancy without a clear editorial reason.

Missing: the command requires a Stacktape API key/login. According to the source (commands.ts), compile-template is NOT in the commandsNotRequiringApiKey list. A first-time user who hasn't run `stacktape login` will hit an auth error with no hint on this page about why.

The 'When to use' section title uses title case while the content begins with 'Compile-template is useful when...' — the section is correct but slightly awkwardly written as a standalone paragraph rather than leading with the command name explicitly.
optionalImprovements: Move the <CliCommandsApiReference> flags table to appear right after the Usage section (and the 1-2 sentence description of what that usage produces), matching the prescribed CLI page structure: intro → usage → reference → examples → related commands.

Fold the 'Important flags' code examples into the 'Examples' section (or drop them) to remove duplication with the flags table. The flags table's own longDescription text already explains these flags; a separate prose section adds friction for readers who just want to look something up.

Add a brief note (one sentence) in the intro or a tip callout that the command requires an active Stacktape login (i.e., `stacktape login` or a configured API key). This prevents a silent auth failure for first-time users who find this page before completing onboarding.

Condense or fold the 'When to use' section into the intro paragraph or a short callout. At two-paragraph length it delays the usage example and adds weight to what should be a lean CLI reference page.

The FAQ answer to 'Why do I need --stage and --region?' mentions '$Secret() references and conditional logic' — consider also mentioning that resource names in the generated CloudFormation include stage as a suffix, which is another reason the stage value changes the template output. This would be a practical, concrete anchor for the reader.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Opening paragraph is accurate and self-contained — grounded in the source implementation (commandRequiresDeployedStack: false, commandModifiesStack: false).
Usage example shows both required flags (--stage, --region) correctly, matching requiredArgs in the source.
Separate fenced blocks per shell command — no copy-button trap violations.
FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS/CloudFormation use-case, and comparison/when-to-use questions.
The 'When to use' bullet list (audit, debug, external tools, diff stages) maps onto realistic scenarios without inventing unsupported capabilities.
Related commands (deploy, preview-changes, package-workloads) are all real commands confirmed in the commandDefinitions source.
problems: FACTUAL ERROR: `templateId` is included in the `sortedArgs` prop of `<CliCommandsApiReference command="compile-template" .../>` but `templateId` is NOT in compile-template's args in the source. It is only in the `init` command. A developer reading this page would believe `--templateId` is a valid compile-template flag, attempt to use it, and get an unknown-argument error.

FACTUAL INACCURACY (FAQ): The FAQ answer to 'What format is the compiled template?' says 'written as YAML by default'. The source implementation unconditionally calls `stringifyToYaml(template)` — there is no format option. Writing 'by default' implies alternatives exist when none do.

STRUCTURAL DEVIATION: The 'Important flags' section manually documents --outFile, --preserveTempFiles, and --configPath before the auto-generated `<CliCommandsApiReference>`. Per the CLI section instructions, the flow should be: intro paragraph → usage → auto-generated reference → examples → related commands. The manual flags section creates duplicate information and breaks the prescribed page rhythm.
mandatoryFixes: Remove `templateId` from the `sortedArgs` array in `<CliCommandsApiReference command="compile-template" .../>`. The `compile-template` command definition in src/config/cli/commands.ts spreads `universalArgs`, `stackArgs`, `configDependentArgs`, plus `outFile` and `preserveTempFiles`. `templateId` is exclusively an `init` command arg and must not appear in compile-template's flags table.

Fix the FAQ answer for 'What format is the compiled template?': change 'written as YAML by default' to 'always written as YAML'. The source in src/commands/compile-template/index.ts calls `stringifyToYaml(template)` unconditionally — there is no flag or option to produce JSON output.
optionalImprovements: Move (or remove) the 'Important flags' section to align with the CLI page section instructions: intro → usage → `<CliCommandsApiReference>` → examples → related. The key flags (--outFile, --preserveTempFiles) are already covered by the auto-generated reference; if callouts are desired, add a short sentence pointing to them after the reference rather than a full manual section before it.

Expand the FAQ toward the recommended 8-10 entries. The current 6 entries are light on AWS/CloudFormation-level questions. Adding 'What is a CloudFormation template?' and 'Can I deploy a compiled template directly with the AWS CLI?' would improve AEO coverage for readers arriving from search.

Consider mentioning `requiresSubscription: false` behavior explicitly — users often wonder whether compile-template counts against a billing tier or requires a logged-in Stacktape account. The source confirms it does not require a subscription, which is worth one sentence.

The prose around `--preserveTempFiles` says files go to `.stacktape/[invocation-id]` — this is sourced from the longDescription in sortedArgs, not from the implementation source. This is fine but worth keeping consistent with the flag's own longDescription already visible in the reference table, to avoid the section feeling like padding.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is self-contained, concrete, and fits the command-reference scope.
Usage examples show the required `--stage` and `--region` flags and keep one command per shell block.
The page uses the required `<CliCommandsApiReference command="compile-template" ... />` component instead of hand-authoring the flags table.

The strongest Stacktape-specific facts are correctly grounded: default `compiled-template.yaml`, optional `--outFile`, required `stage` and `region`, no stack modification, and no deployed-stack requirement.
problems: Some FAQ claims go beyond the provided source, especially that `--stage` and `--region` resolve `$Secret()` references, conditional logic, and region-specific settings. The source only proves those flags are required for the command.
The FAQ claim that the compiled template may reference Stacktape-managed artifacts that only exist after a full deploy is not grounded in the provided `compile-template` command source.
The human-maintained section guidance says the auto-generated reference should come after the usage example and before non-obvious examples; this draft places `Examples` before `Flags reference`.

The `preserveTempFiles` explanation mentions packaged resources and intermediate templates. The generic flag description supports temporary files such as CloudFormation templates and packaged resources, but the `compile-template` implementation only shows template finalization and file writing, so the prose should stay narrower.

The FAQ is useful, but it is mostly Stacktape-specific. For a CLI page that is acceptable, but the CloudFormation/tooling use-case questions could be better separated from Stacktape behavior to improve extractability.
mandatoryFixes: Replace the unsupported explanation of why `--stage` and `--region` are required with a source-grounded version: the command requires both flags, and the compiled output is for that stage and AWS region. Do not claim specific directive, secret, conditional, or region-resolution behavior unless sourced.
Remove or narrow the claim that compiled templates may reference Stacktape-managed artifacts that only exist after `deploy`, unless a source file for packaging/artifact behavior is provided.
Move `<CliCommandsApiReference command="compile-template" ... />` before the non-obvious `Examples` section to match the CLI page section instructions.
optionalImprovements: Rename `Flags reference` to `Reference` or `Arguments` if that matches nearby CLI pages, but the current heading is understandable.

Tighten `Important flags` by making each flag paragraph source-grounded: `--outFile` changes the output path, `--preserveTempFiles` preserves temporary files under `.stacktape/[invocation-id]`, and `--configPath` sets the config file path relative to the current working directory.
Add one concise example for machine-readable use, such as `--agent` or `--outputFormat jsonl`, only if the command actually emits useful structured output for this operation.

Consider adding `package-workloads` comparison language carefully: source supports that `package-workloads` packages compute resources, while `compile-template` compiles the template. Avoid implying a shared artifact lifecycle not visible in source.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the compile-template command source: required flags, default output filename, non-deploying behavior, and the generated CLI reference are broadly consistent. The main risks are a few claims that go beyond the provided command source, especially around locality/authentication, stage/region resolution internals, and packaged artifact behavior.

issues.reason: The source supports that `compile-template` does not modify a stack and does not require a deployed stack, but "strictly local" is broader. The command initializes Stacktape services with global config loaded, and `compile-template` is not listed among commands that do not require an API key, so the draft should not imply a purely local/no-auth operation from the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with: "The `compile-template` command does not modify a deployed stack and does not require an existing deployed stack."

evidence.quote:
// Commands that don't require API key
export const commandsNotRequiringApiKey: StacktapeCommand[] = [
  'login',
  'logout',
  'mcp',
  'mcp:add',
  'version',
  'help',
  'defaults:list',
  'defaults:configure',
  'upgrade',
  'init'
];

issues.statement: FAQ: "Stacktape uses the stage and region to resolve stage-specific configuration values (like [`$Secret()`](/configuration/directives) references and conditional logic) and to determine region-specific resource settings."
issues.reason: The provided source shows only that `stage` and `region` are required arguments and that resources are resolved before finalizing the template. It does not document `$Secret()` resolution, conditional logic, or region-specific setting behavior for this command.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to the sourced fact: "`--stage` and `--region` are required because Stacktape compiles the template for a specific stage and AWS region."

issues.statement: Manual prose says `--preserveTempFiles` keeps "packaged resources, intermediate templates" for compile-template, and the FAQ says compiled templates may reference Stacktape-managed artifacts "that only exist after a full deploy."
issues.reason: The compile-template implementation shown in the source resolves resources, finalizes the template, writes the YAML file, and returns the template. Packaging compute artifacts is documented as the purpose of the separate `package-workloads` command. The draft should not claim compile-template preserves packaged resources or that artifacts only exist after full deploy unless additional source supports that lifecycle.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the packaged-artifact details from the compile-template page, or narrow them to: "`--preserveTempFiles` preserves temporary files generated by the operation." In the FAQ, say external standalone CloudFormation deployment is not documented as a supported workflow here.

issues.statement: "By default Stacktape looks for `stacktape.ts` (or `stacktape.yml`) in the current directory."
issues.reason: The provided compile-template source and generated CLI reference do not state the default configuration filename discovery behavior. The `configPath` argument is present, but its default lookup rules are not grounded in the supplied files.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep this to the sourced behavior: "Use `--configPath` to point to a config file outside the default lookup path." If the page needs default filename behavior, cite the config-loading source in the page source list.

positiveFindings: The default output file claim is grounded by both `src/config/cli/commands.ts` and `src/commands/compile-template/index.ts`.
The required `--stage` and `--region` flags match the command definition.
The draft correctly uses one command per shell block and links only to routes present in the provided navigation index.
The draft does not include forbidden hand-authored Stacktape YAML or fenced TypeScript Stacktape config examples.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the command definition and runtime implementation. The main factual risks are unsupported claims about standalone CloudFormation reuse, artifact existence, CI exit-code behavior, and what stage/region resolution does.

issues.statement: “The output is a valid CloudFormation template, but it may reference Stacktape-managed artifacts (Lambda zips in S3, container images in ECR) that only exist after a full deploy.”
issues.reason: The compile-template implementation finalizes and writes a CloudFormation template, but the provided sources do not state that the compiled template may reference deploy-created Lambda zips, ECR images, or artifacts that only exist after a full deploy. That detail would materially affect whether a reader can use the template outside Stacktape.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the answer to sourced behavior: the command writes the finalized CloudFormation template and is intended for inspection. Avoid claiming deploy-only artifact references unless a packaging/deploy source is added.

issues.statement: “Stacktape uses the stage and region to resolve stage-specific configuration values (like `$Secret()` references and conditional logic) and to determine region-specific resource settings.”
issues.reason: The sources show `stage` and `region` are required for the command, but they do not document the specific resolution behavior for `$Secret()`, conditional logic, or region-specific resource settings. This explanation may be true elsewhere, but it is not grounded in the provided compile-template sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Say only that `--stage` and `--region` are required arguments for `compile-template`; if explaining why, phrase cautiously: “Stacktape requires the same stage and region inputs used by deployment-time stack operations.”

issues.statement: “Can I run compile-template in CI? Yes. The command works in non-interactive environments and exits with a zero status code on success. Use `--outputFormat plain` or `--agent` for CI-friendly output.”
issues.reason: The provided compile-template runtime source does not state CI behavior, non-interactive guarantees, or exit-code behavior. The command args include universal arguments, but the source shown does not ground the specific CI recommendation or success exit-code claim.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the exit-code and CI-behavior guarantee, or narrow it to a generic usage example: “You can run `compile-template` before other CI checks by passing the required `--stage` and `--region` flags.”

issues.statement: “`--preserveTempFiles` keeps the intermediate files Stacktape generates during compilation (packaged resources, intermediate templates) in `.stacktape/[invocation-id]`.”
issues.reason: The command accepts `preserveTempFiles`, but the provided compile-template sources do not show what files are preserved for this command or whether compilation produces packaged resources. The generated flag text in the draft may come from shared option metadata, but the included source list does not ground the command-specific examples.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the more cautious shared flag wording: “`--preserveTempFiles` preserves temporary files generated by the operation in `.stacktape/[invocation-id]`.” Avoid naming packaged resources unless supported by command-specific source.

issues.statement: “By default Stacktape looks for `stacktape.ts` (or `stacktape.yml`) in the current directory.”
issues.reason: The compile-template command description in the provided source mentions `stacktape.yml` in other command prose and the `configPath` arg exists, but no provided source states the default config discovery order or that `stacktape.ts` is looked up before `stacktape.yml`.
issues.suggestedFix: Say “Use `--configPath` to point to a config file outside the default discovery path” or cite config-discovery source if available.

positiveFindings: The required usage example includes both required flags, `--stage` and `--region`, matching `requiredArgs: ['stage', 'region']`.
The default output filename `compiled-template.yaml` and `--outFile` override are grounded in both the command definition and runtime implementation.
The claim that the command does not require an existing deployed stack is supported by `commandRequiresDeployedStack: false`.
The page uses `<CliCommandsApiReference command="compile-template" ... />`, which matches the documented CLI page requirement.

verifierResults.summary: The page largely follows the CLI-reference structure: one command, required flags shown in usage examples, an auto-generated flags reference, and related commands. I found one medium issue where a commonly used path-resolution flag is present only in the API reference and not explained in the guide prose.

issues.statement: The page explains `--configPath` but does not explain `--currentWorkingDirectory` outside the generated flags table.
issues.reason: For a command that compiles local configuration and writes a local template, the working directory is a practical, frequently relevant option because it controls how file paths in the configuration are resolved. The draft discusses non-default config paths, but omits the adjacent path-resolution behavior that users commonly need in monorepos or scripts.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short paragraph in “Important flags” explaining that `--currentWorkingDirectory` sets the working directory for the operation and that file paths in the configuration are resolved relative to it. Include a single command example if useful.

evidence.quote: 'compile-template': { ... args: { ...universalArgs, ...stackArgs, ...configDependentArgs, outFile: outFile.optional(), preserveTempFiles: preserveTempFiles.optional() }, requiredArgs: ['stage', 'region'] as const
**`--configPath`** points to a non-default config file location. By default Stacktape looks for `stacktape.ts` (or `stacktape.yml`) in the current directory.

positiveFindings: The draft uses exactly one `<CliCommandsApiReference command="compile-template" ... />` component and does not hand-author the flags table.
All Stacktape CLI examples include the required `--stage` and `--region` flags for `compile-template`.
The documented default output file, `compiled-template.yaml`, is grounded in both the command definition and implementation.

The page correctly states that the command does not modify a deployed stack and does not require an existing deployed stack, matching `commandModifiesStack: false` and `commandRequiresDeployedStack: false` in the implementation.
No Stacktape configuration examples are present, so the page avoids the forbidden YAML and plain-object config patterns.
